schools.summary: Gill: 'principalities and powers' are not angels (good or bad, sometimes so called) but men in high places, the higher powers ordained of God, which the Apostle Peter distinguishes into the king as supreme and the governors under him (the Roman emperor and senate); Paul charging that the believers be put in mind to be subject to principalities and powers, to obey magistrates, and to be ready to every good work — the duty of Christian submission to government and readiness for good (Paul, turning to the believers' conduct in the world, bidding Titus 'put them in mind,' remind them of a known duty, to 'be subject to principalities and powers,' the civil rulers and authorities ordained of God, 'to obey magistrates,' yielding lawful obedience to those in office, and 'to be ready to every good work,' prompt and willing for all that is good, so Christians, far from being seditious, are to be model subjects, obedient to government and forward in every good deed).

themes: put them in mind to be subject to principalities and powers
to obey magistrates
to be ready to every good work
Christians model subjects, obedient and good

schools.summary: Gill: they are to speak evil of no man — not of one another, nor of the men of the world to the prejudice of their names and characters (which are tender things, to be gently touched), nor of magistrates, principalities, and powers, of persons in dignity and authority, which the false teachers were not afraid to speak evil of; Paul charging that they speak evil of no man, be no brawlers, but gentle, showing all meekness unto all men — the peaceable, gentle, and meek temper required toward everyone (Paul completing the believers' social duty with a charge to a gracious spirit toward all: 'to speak evil of no man,' no slandering or reviling of anyone ruler or neighbour, 'to be no brawlers,' not contentious or quarrelsome, 'but gentle,' mild and equitable, yielding rather than fierce, 'shewing all meekness unto all men,' a humble, patient, gentle temper toward every person friend or foe, so the Christian is to be marked by a peaceable and gentle disposition in all his dealings).

themes: to speak evil of no man, to be no brawlers
but gentle
shewing all meekness unto all men
a gracious, peaceable temper toward everyone

schools.summary: Gill: nothing has a greater tendency to promote humility and check pride in the saints than to reflect upon their past state and condition, what they themselves once were; and this is a reason why magistrates, though evil men, should be obeyed in things good and lawful, and why no man should be spoken evil of; Paul reminding that we ourselves were once foolish, disobedient, deceived, serving lusts and pleasures, living in malice and envy, hateful and hating one another — the humbling memory of what we once were as the ground of gentleness (Paul giving the reason for the gentleness toward all just enjoined, the remembrance of our own past: 'for we ourselves also were sometimes' all this, 'foolish,' without spiritual understanding, 'disobedient' to God, 'deceived,' led astray by error, 'serving divers lusts and pleasures,' slaves to our appetites, 'living in malice and envy, hateful, and hating one another,' full of ill-will and mutual hatred, so that, remembering we were once no better than the worst we now meet, we have every reason to deal gently with others, our own former state forbidding all pride).

themes: for we ourselves also were sometimes foolish, disobedient, deceived
serving divers lusts and pleasures
living in malice and envy, hateful, and hating one another
the humbling memory of our former state

schools.summary: Gill: this was 'after' all that series and course of wickedness, notwithstanding all that foolishness, disobedience, deception, bondage to sin, envy, malice, and malignity; or 'when' all this was (as the word may be rendered), amid all this iniquity, when these persons were in the full career of sin and so had done no preparatory works; Paul declaring that, after that the kindness and love of God our Saviour toward man appeared, the great change came — the free kindness of God breaking in upon our sin (Paul, having drawn the dark picture of what we were, setting against it the bright 'but' of grace: 'after that the kindness and love of God our Saviour toward man appeared,' when, amid all that wickedness with no preparatory works on our part, the 'kindness,' benignity, and 'love toward man,' philanthropy, God's love to mankind, of God our Saviour shone forth in the gospel, so the whole change in us sprang not from anything in us but from the free, spontaneous kindness of God, breaking in upon our sin).

themes: but after that the kindness and love of God our Saviour
toward man appeared
the free kindness of God breaking in upon our sin
the change from God, not from us

schools.summary: Gill: 'works of righteousness' are works done according to a righteous law, in obedience to it, and in a righteous manner; salvation is denied to be brought about by any works, even the best works of men; the great instance of the kindness and love of God our Saviour is salvation, according to his mercy, by the washing of regeneration (grace comparable to water) and the renewing of the Holy Ghost; Paul declaring that not by works of righteousness which we have done, but according to his mercy God saved us, by the washing of regeneration and renewing of the Holy Ghost — the salvation that is wholly of mercy not works, wrought by the renewing Spirit (Paul stating the ground of salvation with the fullest clearness: 'not by works of righteousness which we have done,' not by any deeds of ours however good as cause or condition, 'but according to his mercy he saved us,' the sole moving cause being God's free mercy, and naming the means by which that mercy works in us, 'the washing of regeneration, and renewing of the Holy Ghost,' the inward cleansing and new birth, the renewing work of the Spirit who makes us new creatures).

debates.question: On what ground are we saved, and what is 'the washing of regeneration'?

sides.position: Saved not by our works but solely according to God's mercy; the saving work is the Spirit's regenerating and renewing of the heart
'The washing of regeneration' is read by some as pointing to baptism as the sign of the inward renewing the Spirit works (which it signifies, not which the water effects)

themes: not by works of righteousness which we have done
but according to his mercy he saved us
by the washing of regeneration, and renewing of the Holy Ghost
salvation wholly of mercy, wrought by the Spirit

schools.summary: Gill: this means either the love of God shed abroad in the hearts of those whom he regenerated and renewed by his Spirit, or the water of regeneration (grace comparable to water) plentifully shed and caused to abound where sin had abounded, or rather the Holy Spirit himself, with his gifts and graces, shed on them abundantly; Paul declaring that God shed the Holy Spirit on us abundantly through Jesus Christ our Saviour — the rich outpouring of the renewing Spirit given through Christ (Paul, having named the renewing of the Holy Ghost, telling how richly that Spirit is given: God 'shed,' poured out, the Spirit 'on us abundantly,' richly and plenteously not in scant measure, and that 'through Jesus Christ our Saviour,' for it is by and through the mediation of Christ that the Spirit is bestowed on his people, so the very fountain of the new life, the Holy Spirit, is given not sparingly but in rich abundance, purchased and channelled through the Saviour).

themes: which he shed on us abundantly
through Jesus Christ our Saviour
the rich outpouring of the renewing Spirit
given through the mediation of Christ

schools.summary: Gill: this is another way and means, as well as regeneration, by which God saves his people, for he saves no unjustified ones — no unrighteous persons shall inherit the kingdom of heaven, and such as are without the wedding garment and robe of Christ's righteousness shall be cast into outer darkness, but whom God saves he justifies; Paul declaring that, being justified by his grace, we should be made heirs according to the hope of eternal life — the justification by grace that makes us heirs of eternal life (Paul naming the end and fruit of all this saving work: 'being justified by his grace,' accounted righteous before God freely, not by our works but by his grace through the righteousness of Christ, the purpose was that 'we should be made heirs,' adopted into God's family as sons and heirs, 'according to the hope of eternal life,' heirs of the very glory hoped for, so the same free grace that regenerates and justifies also makes the believer an heir of eternal life, the whole of salvation first to last being of grace).

themes: that being justified by his grace
we should be made heirs according to the hope of eternal life
justification by grace, not works
grace making the believer an heir of eternal life

schools.summary: Chrysostom: having spoken of the love of God to man, of his ineffable regard for us, of what we were and what he has done for us, Paul adds 'these things I will that thou affirm constantly, that they which have believed in God might be careful to maintain good works' — that is, discourse of these things and from a consideration of them exhort to almsgiving and every good deed; Paul declaring it a faithful saying, to be affirmed constantly, that believers be careful to maintain good works — the constant affirming of saving grace that it may produce good works (Paul sealing the whole passage on salvation by grace as 'a faithful saying,' certainly true and to be relied on, and charging Titus to 'affirm constantly,' keep insisting on, these truths, and that to a practical end: 'that they which have believed in God might be careful to maintain good works,' that the doctrine of free grace, far from making men careless, might rouse the justified to a diligent, studious pursuit of good works out of gratitude, 'these things are good and profitable unto men,' so the preaching of grace and the pressing of good works go together).

themes: this is a faithful saying
that they which have believed in God might be careful to maintain good works
the constant affirming of grace that produces good works
grace rousing the justified to diligent good

schools.summary: Gill: the 'foolish questions' are such as were started in the schools of the Jews; Matthew Henry: here is the fifth and last thing in the matter of the epistle — what Titus should avoid in teaching, how he should deal with a heretic, with some other directions; Paul charging that Titus avoid foolish questions, genealogies, contentions, and strivings about the law, as unprofitable and vain — the rejection of fruitless controversy in favour of edifying truth (Paul, having pressed the maintaining of good works, warning against their great hindrance and the opposite of profitable teaching: 'foolish questions,' silly speculations, 'genealogies,' idle Jewish tracings of descent, 'contentions, and strivings about the law,' wranglings over the ceremonial law, all of which 'are unprofitable and vain,' yielding no good and building up no one, so Titus is to turn from barren controversy to the sound, edifying truths that produce godliness).

themes: but avoid foolish questions, and genealogies
and contentions, and strivings about the law
for they are unprofitable and vain
turning from barren controversy to edifying truth

schools.summary: Gill: a heretic, according to the notation of the word, is either one that makes choice of an opinion upon his own judgment, contrary to the generally received sense of the churches of Christ, and prefers it to theirs, and obstinately persists in it, separates from them, forms a party, and sets himself at the head of them; Paul charging that a man who is a heretic, after the first and second admonition, be rejected — the orderly dealing with the obstinate heretic (Paul giving the rule for the divisive errorist: 'a man that is an heretick,' one who chooses and persists in error, breaking the church's unity, is not to be cut off rashly but dealt with patiently first, 'after the first and second admonition,' after he has been warned once and again and refuses to repent, he is to be 'reject[ed],' shunned, put away, refused further fellowship, so discipline is to be exercised but only after due and patient warning, the obstinate alone being finally rejected).

themes: a man that is an heretick
after the first and second admonition reject
the orderly dealing with the obstinate heretic
discipline only after due and patient warning

schools.summary: Gill: he that is such is 'subverted' — overturned and demolished, like an edifice that is not only decaying and falling but entirely everted and pulled down, so that there is no hope of a restoration or recovery; he is in a desperate condition, having opposed the person, or office, or sacrifice of Christ; Paul declaring that such a one is subverted, sinneth, and is condemned of himself — the heretic's ruin and self-conviction justifying his rejection (Paul giving the reason the obstinate heretic may justly be rejected: 'knowing that he that is such is subverted,' utterly overturned in the foundations of his faith, ruined past hope, 'and sinneth,' persists wilfully in his sin, 'being condemned of himself,' self-condemned, for his own conscience convicts him, knowing the truth he forsakes, so his rejection is no harshness, the man having pronounced sentence on himself by persisting against the light).

themes: knowing that he that is such is subverted, and sinneth
being condemned of himself
the heretic's ruin and self-conviction
his rejection no harshness but just

schools.summary: Gill: Artemas and Tychicus were both ministers of the gospel; there is no mention of Artemas anywhere else (some say he was one of the seventy disciples and afterwards bishop of Lystra, but these are uncertain, the name a contraction of Artemidorus), while Tychicus is often spoken of as a faithful and beloved minister; Paul saying that when he sends Artemas or Tychicus, Titus should come to him at Nicopolis, where he had determined to winter — the personal arrangements for Titus to rejoin Paul (Paul, turning to personal matters, letting Titus know his plans: he will send one of two trusted ministers, 'Artemas or Tychicus,' to take up the work in Crete in Titus's place, and then Titus is to 'be diligent to come,' make haste to come, to Paul at 'Nicopolis,' where Paul had 'determined there to winter,' so the work would not be left undone when Titus departed, the apostle providing a substitute and arranging the reunion).

themes: when I shall send Artemas unto thee, or Tychicus
be diligent to come unto me to Nicopolis
for I have determined there to winter
a substitute provided so the work not be left undone

schools.summary: Gill: whether Zenas was brought up to the civil law among the Greeks or Romans is uncertain; it may be he was a Jewish lawyer or scribe, an interpreter of Moses's law among the Jews, for with them a lawyer and a scribe were one and the same (the Syriac version here calls him 'a scribe'); Paul charging Titus to bring Zenas the lawyer and Apollos on their journey diligently, that nothing be wanting to them — the duty of generous hospitality to travelling ministers (Paul commending to Titus's care two servants of the gospel passing through, 'Zenas the lawyer and Apollos,' the latter the eloquent and learned Apollos known elsewhere, charging that he 'bring them on their journey diligently,' help them forward on their way with care and generosity, 'that nothing be wanting unto them,' so that they lack nothing needful for the journey, so the believers are to provide liberally for the needs of travelling ministers of Christ).

themes: bring Zenas the lawyer and Apollos on their journey diligently
that nothing be wanting unto them
the duty of generous hospitality to travelling ministers
providing liberally for the gospel's servants

schools.summary: Gill: by 'good works' are meant not only honest trades, as some render the words (it is true a trade is a work, and an honest lawful employment of life is a good work which ought to be maintained, attended to, followed, and learnt in order to be followed), but the broader doing of good for necessary uses; Paul charging that believers also learn to maintain good works for necessary uses, that they be not unfruitful — the call for all the saints to be diligent in good and useful works (Paul, from the case of helping Zenas and Apollos, drawing a general lesson for all: 'let ours also,' our people, all the believers, 'learn to maintain good works,' give themselves to the practice of good, honest labour and works of charity, 'for necessary uses,' to meet the real needs that arise such as the relief of the saints and travelling ministers, and that to a clear end, 'that they be not unfruitful,' that no believer's life be barren and useless but every one fruitful in good).

themes: and let ours also learn to maintain good works
for necessary uses
that they be not unfruitful
every believer diligent in good and useful works

schools.summary: Gill: all the apostles, fellow labourers, and ministers of the gospel that were with Paul, and all the members of the church where he was, sent their Christian salutation to Titus, who was a person greatly esteemed and whose praise was in all the churches; Paul sending greetings from all with him, bidding Titus greet them that love them in the faith, and closing with the benediction of grace — the parting salutations and blessing that close the epistle (Paul ending with the warm exchange of Christian love: 'all that are with me salute thee,' all his companions sending their greetings to Titus so esteemed among them, and bidding Titus in turn 'greet them that love us in the faith,' the believers in Crete who love Paul and his fellows in the bond of the common faith, and sealing the whole letter with his accustomed benediction, 'Grace be with you all. Amen,' invoking the free favour of God upon Titus and all the saints with him).

themes: all that are with me salute thee
greet them that love us in the faith
Grace be with you all. Amen
the parting salutations and blessing of grace
